title: Working with parts that hold religious or spiritual trauma
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The part of you that still fears the fire 

 I remember sitting in a pew at 14, sweating through my Sunday dress, convinced that God could see every singl...
htmlContent:
The part of you that still fears the fire

I remember sitting in a pew at 14, sweating through my Sunday dress, convinced that God could see every single thought I had. Every doubt. Every question. Every moment my mind wandered during the sermon. I was terrified. Not of my parents finding out, but of something far bigger. Something that knew me completely and could reject me forever.

That part of me is still in there. She shows up sometimes when I hear certain hymns in a grocery store, or when someone talks about prayer with too much certainty. My throat tightens. My chest gets heavy. And I feel that old shame wash over me like a wave.

If you carry religious or spiritual trauma, you know exactly what I'm talking about. It's a specific kind of wound. One that gets tangled up with your sense of worth, your belonging, your very existence. And here's the thing: the parts of you that formed around that trauma are not your enemy. They're not broken. They're not wrong for existing. They're doing exactly what they learned to do to survive.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not just the mind. And religious trauma? It gets deep into the nervous system. It's not just a set of beliefs you can reason your way out of. It's a felt sense of being watched, judged, and found lacking. That's a body-level experience. A nervous system response.

So when I work with the part of me that still fears hell, or the part that still tries to earn love through perfection, I don't argue with her. I don't tell her she's wrong. I sit with her. I let her tell me what she's afraid of. And I remind myself that she formed in a specific context, at a specific time, to protect me from something that felt genuinely life-threatening.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It helped me see how the absence of emotional safety in religious environments creates its own kind of wound.

What religious trauma does to the nervous system

Let's get specific about what happens biologically. When you grow up in a high-control religious environment, your nervous system learns to scan for danger constantly. Is God watching? Am I being good enough? Will I be rejected if I fail? That hypervigilance doesn't go away just because you leave the church.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that your nervous system got stuck in a survival state. Your body learned that safety was conditional. That connection could be withdrawn at any moment. That love required performance. And that's not a belief you can just think your way out of. It's a pattern in your autonomic nervous system.

Think about it. When you hear a worship song, does your body relax or tighten? When someone mentions sin, do you feel shame in your gut? When you pray, do you feel peace or pressure? Those are not intellectual responses. They're nervous system responses. And they're held by specific parts of you that learned those patterns long ago.

I have a part that still tries to be perfect. She shows up when I'm about to publish something or when I'm in a conflict with someone I care about. She whispers that if I'm good enough, if I say the right things, if I don't make anyone angry, then I'll be safe. Then I'll be loved. Then I won't be abandoned.

That part didn't come from nowhere. She came from a religious system that taught me that God's love was conditional, that I had to earn my place, that my worth depended on my behavior. And she's been working overtime for decades to keep me safe in a world that felt dangerous.

Wild, right? That the very thing that helped you survive is now the thing that keeps you stuck?

Meeting the exile with curiosity instead of fear

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), talks about exiles. These are the parts of us that carry the most pain, the shame, the fear, the grief. They're the parts we try to push away because they hurt too much. But here's the paradox: the more we push them away, the more they control us.

For people with religious trauma, the exiles often carry things like:

- The fear of eternal punishment
- The shame of being flawed
- The grief of losing community and belonging
- The anger at being deceived or controlled
- The loneliness of doubting when everyone around you seemed so certain

These parts are not weak. They're not broken. They're incredibly strong, actually. They've been carrying this weight for years, sometimes decades, with no one to help them. And they're desperate for someone to finally see them, to finally hear them, to finally say, "I see you. I'm sorry you went through that. You didn't deserve it."

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name how trauma actually works in people's lives, including the kind that comes from systems and institutions.

When I work with my own religious trauma parts, I try to approach them with curiosity. Not judgment. Not fixing. Just curiosity. I ask them: What are you afraid would happen if you stopped trying so hard? What do you need me to know about what you went through? What would it feel like to rest for a moment?

And sometimes they answer. Not in words, exactly. But in sensations. A loosening in my chest. A tear that comes from nowhere. A deep sigh that I didn't know I was holding.

That's the part finally being heard. That's the beginning of healing.

Navigating the protector parts that keep you safe from spirituality

There's another layer to this work. The protectors. These are the parts that formed after the trauma to make sure you never get hurt that way again. They might be cynical about anything spiritual. They might reject all forms of community or ritual. They might get angry when anyone mentions God or faith or prayer.

And honestly? They have good reason. They saw what happened last time. They're not going to let you get fooled again.

But here's the thing about protectors: they're also parts. They have their own fears, their own histories, their own pain. And when you push them away or judge them as "too closed off" or "too bitter," you're doing the same thing to them that was done to you. You're rejecting a part of yourself that's trying to help.

I have a protector part that absolutely cannot stand anything that smells like spiritual manipulation. She's sharp. She's skeptical. She's got a list of every way religion has hurt people, and she's ready to recite it at a moment's notice. And I love her. She's kept me safe from a lot of harmful situations. She's helped me see through bullshit that I might have swallowed whole in my younger years.

But she also blocks me from experiences that might actually be healing. Meditation. Somatic practices. Community rituals that aren't about control. She sees the shape of something familiar and assumes it's the same poison. And I get that. I really do.

So I work with her. I thank her for protecting me. I ask her what she needs to feel safe enough to let me try something new. And sometimes she says no, and that's okay. Other times she says yes, but only if we go slow, only if we keep our eyes open, only if we leave the second it feels wrong.

That's the negotiation of healing. It's not about forcing yourself to be spiritual again. It's not about rejecting all of it. It's about finding what's actually true for you, what actually helps you feel more alive and connected, without the control and shame and fear.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She writes from a Buddhist-informed perspective that doesn't require any particular belief system, just a willingness to meet yourself with compassion.

Building a new relationship with the sacred on your own terms

This is the part that doesn't get talked about enough. After religious trauma, you might not want anything to do with spirituality at all. And that's completely valid. You don't have to find your way back to anything. You don't have to reclaim anything. You don't have to make peace with anything.

But some of us find that we miss something. Not the control. Not the fear. Not the shame. But the sense of connection. The awe. The feeling of being part of something larger than ourselves. The community. The ritual. The silence that feels full instead of empty.

And that's where the work gets really interesting. Because you get to rebuild from scratch. You get to decide what feels true to you. You get to experiment with practices that feel safe and nourishing, without any authority figure telling you you're doing it wrong.

For me, that's looked like:

- Sitting outside and just noticing the trees without trying to find a lesson in it
- Breathing deeply and feeling my body without praying for forgiveness
- Being in community with people who don't require me to believe the same things they do
- Reading poetry that touches something in me without needing to be "inspired"
- Letting myself grieve what I lost without rushing to find meaning in it

This is slow work. It's not linear. Some days I feel connected and whole. Other days I feel angry and closed off. Both are okay. Both are part of the process.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you actual exercises to work with, which can be grounding when you feel lost in the abstract.

I want to be honest with you about something. This work doesn't end. You don't wake up one day and never feel the old shame again. The parts don't disappear. They get lighter. They get softer. They trust you more. But they're still there, and they still need your attention sometimes.

And that's not failure. That's just what it means to be human. To carry history in your body. To have parts that remember. To keep them anyway.

Sit with that.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something shift? Did something tighten? Did a part of you want to argue, or cry, or run away?

That's the work. Right there. Not in some future where you're finally healed. But in this moment, with this part, in this body, right now.

You're not alone in this. The parts you carry from religious trauma are not your fault. They're not a sign of weakness. They're evidence of what you survived. And they deserve your compassion, your curiosity, and your patience.

One step at a time. One breath at a time. One part at a time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect specifically
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook for C-PTSD survivors
